ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 11713004 refers to water as a substance; used as closest parent since no specific bottled-water product code exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 500 ml bottle of water, as typically served at a Japanese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Water, tap, drinking — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174831)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain drinking water nutrient profile used. Bottled still water has an identical macro profile to tap water: 0 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. USDA FDC SR Legacy entry 174831 confirms all core macros and secondary nutrients are zero or trace. Sodium value of 4 mg per 100 g is taken from USDA FDC entry for bottled water (FDC ID 174718) to reflect typical mineral content of commercially bottled water.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure water contains no glycemic carbohydrates and therefore has no measurable glycemic index. GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value per Veritas convention) to satisfy numeric field requirements. GL per 500 ml serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is 0. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database convention that water is not assigned a GI value due to absence of carbohydrate.

hypertension.guidance: Bottled water contains approximately 4 mg sodium per 100 ml, which is negligible and poses no concern for individuals managing hypertension; adequate daily water intake is recommended for blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Bottled water contains no carbohydrates, produces no postprandial glucose response, and is the preferred hydration choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bottled water is free of saturated fat, cholesterol, and excess sodium, making it a cardiovascularly neutral and recommended hydration source for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bottled water is commercially packaged still drinking water sold in sealed plastic or glass bottles. It contains no calories, no macronutrients, and no glycemic carbohydrates, making it the most nutritionally neutral beverage available. Bottled water may contain trace minerals such as calcium, magnesium, and potassium depending on the source (spring, mineral, or purified), but these are present in negligible quantities relative to dietary requirements. In the context of a Japanese restaurant in Singapore, bottled water is commonly offered as a still water option alongside sparkling water and other beverages. It is suitable for all dietary patterns including vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. Clinically, bottled water is the preferred hydration choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease, as it contributes no sugar, sodium load, or calories. Adequate hydration supports metabolic function, kidney health, and blood pressure regulation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bottled water?
Is bottled water good for diabetics?
Can I drink bottled water if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Bottled water is a universally defined food item with a well-established nutrient profile of zero macronutrients. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy provides exact nutrient data confirming all core macros are zero. No estimation or analogy is required for nutrient values.
notes: energy_kcal is naturally zero as bottled water contains no caloric macronutrients. protein_g is naturally zero as water contains no protein. fat_g is naturally zero as water contains no fat. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as water contains no carbohydrates. sugar_g is naturally zero. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero. All vitamin and most mineral values are confirmed zero or trace per USDA FDC. sodium_mg of 4.0 mg per 100 g reflects typical commercially bottled water mineral content per USDA FDC ID 174718. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are confirmed zero per USDA FDC for plain water. The food_name in the input ('1005D Bottle Water (D)') appears to be a restaurant menu code; canonical English name 'Bottled Water' is used. Cuisine tag 'Japanese' is retained from the source restaurant context (GyoGyo - Funan, a Japanese restaurant in Singapore) but bottled water is a globally universal product with no specific cuisine of origin.

improvement_actions_taken: Replaced menu-code food_name '1005D Bottle Water (D)' with canonical English name 'Bottled Water'.
Added Japanese local name ボトルウォーター per G6 mandatory rule for Japanese cuisine.
Set all core macros to 0.0 with 'naturally zero' justification phrases in notes per zero-macro justification rule.
Set glycemic_index to 1 and gi_category to 'low' per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule.
Documented all zero values with appropriate justification phrases.
Ensured aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: exact, closest_parent, none.
Provided full health context for all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance.
